This thesis investigates beta regression models with random effects for the analysis of scale data, such as those obtained through instruments like the Numerical Rating Scale (NRS) and Likert scales, widely used in medical and social science domains. Extensions of the classical beta regression models are proposed through the incorporation of random effects, allowing the modeling of correlated and hierarchically structured data. Inference is carried out via pseudo-likelihood-based methods, with integration via adaptive Gauss-Hermite quadrature and quasi-Monte Carlo methods. Simulation studies assessed the performance of the estimators under various scenarios. Applications to real pain assessment data demonstrate the practical utility of the proposed methods. All methods have been implemented in the publicly available R package \texttt{betaregscale}.
